nativeRange.habitat: Coastal and sheltered reef flats, rocky reef bottoms; found at depths of approximately 5–30 m, typically in small groups

diet.notes: Primarily algae-based; has been documented grazing benthic algae and macroalgae in the wild. In captivity, dried seaweed (nori) offered on a clip tends to be accepted readily and is generally advised as a dietary staple. Spirulina, algae-based pellets, and occasional Mysis shrimp have been reported to be accepted. Regular algae feeding tends to help maintain color and reduce aggression toward tankmates.

avoidWith: other tangs and surgeonfish (especially same genus)
small fish that may be intimidated

summary: A large, striking tang from the Indian Ocean known for its golden-tan body and vivid orange caudal peduncle spines. Generally considered reef-safe and peaceful toward non-tang tankmates; tends to be aggressive toward other surgeonfish.
careNotes: Naso elegans is the Indian Ocean form of the orange-spine unicornfish, distinct from the Pacific Naso lituratus despite frequent trade-name confusion; the two species share similar care requirements but differ in coloration and geographic origin. Juveniles display a gray body with yellow dorsal and anal fin stripes; adults develop a darker bluish-gray tone, prominent orange caudal peduncle keels, and males develop long lyre-shaped tail streamers. This species has been noted to be susceptible to ich (Cryptocaryon irritans) and marine velvet (Amyloodinium), likely due to a relatively thin mucus coat compared to some other fish — quarantine and stable water quality are generally advised. Caudal peduncle keels (scalpels) are permanently erect and can cause injury to handlers and net material; care during catching and handling has been widely recommended. Escape (jumping) behavior has been documented across multiple sources; a tight-fitting lid is considered essential. Tank size: sources range from 180 gal (~680 L) recommended by mainstream hobby sources to 900–1000 L recommended by specialist reef aquarists; the averaged figure used here (~780 L) reflects the midpoint, and a larger tank is generally preferred for long-term keeping of this active, large-growing fish. Temperature: FishBase wild-range data (24.8–28.7°C) is somewhat higher than typical hobby recommendations (22–26°C); the range used here (23–27°C) averages these figures. Protogynous hermaphroditism has not been documented for Naso elegans in reviewed sources; this trait is more characteristic of wrasses and some angelfishes. Captive breeding has not been reported; all specimens in the trade are wild-caught.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been reported for this species. Wild populations are presumed broadcast spawners. Males are distinguishable from females by the development of long caudal-fin streamers upon maturity.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Product page for Blonde Naso Tang (Naso elegans). Provided adult size, tank size, temperature, pH, salinity, diet, temperament, reef compatibility, and care level.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name, taxonomy (family Acanthuridae, order Acanthuriformes), adult size (max 45 cm SL), native range (Indian Ocean: Red Sea to Durban, east to southwestern Indonesia), wild temperature range (24.8–28.7°C), depth range (5–30 m), and diet (herbivore, benthic algae, trophic level 2.0). FishBase temperature ceiling (28.7°C) is higher than most hobby sources; the used range (23–27°C) reflects an average across all sources.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (18 in = ~45 cm), min tank size (180 gal = ~681 L), temperature (75–79°F = 23.9–26.1°C), salinity (1.020–1.025), pH (8.1–8.4), native range, and escape/jumping behavior.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (45 cm), min tank size (900 L — higher than mainstream hobby sources, averaged into the final figure), reef safety (always reef-safe), and escape/jumping risk (documented jumper from open aquaria).

additional.name: Light.fish
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (18 in), min tank size (200 gal = ~757 L), temperature (72–78°F), lifespan (10+ years captivity, up to 30 years wild), escape behavior, and breeding notes (all specimens wild-caught).

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been reported; all trade specimens are wild-caught. Wild reproduction is assumed to involve broadcast spawning of pelagic eggs. Males develop pronounced caudal-fin streamers that help distinguish sexes in mature individuals.
